title: Low moments of character sums
speaker: Adam Harper
abstract: Sums of Dirichlet characters ∑n≤xχ(n)∑n≤xχ(n) (where χχ is a character modulo some prime rr, say) are one of the best studied objects in analytic number theory. Their size is the subject of numerous results and conjectures, such as the Pólya-Vinogradov inequality and the Burgess bound. One way to get information about this is to study the power moments 1r−1∑χ mod r|∑n≤xχ(n)|2q1r−1∑χ mod r|∑n≤xχ(n)|2q, which turns out to be quite a subtle question that connects with issues in probability and physics.

proof_stages.label: Dirichlet-character cancellation and moment background
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Dirichlet characters modulo a large prime, recalls classical cancellation bounds and average moment results, and formulates the question whether typical character sums really have square-root size.

proof_stages.label: Low-moment theorem for character sums
proof_stages.summary: Harper states the upper bound for the 2q-th moments, 0<q<1, with saving governed by l=min(x,r/x), and explains that for growing x and r/x the first moment is smaller than root x by a log-log factor.

proof_stages.label: Steinhaus random multiplicative model
proof_stages.summary: A random completely multiplicative function with independent Steinhaus prime values is introduced, its exact orthogonality and mean-square analogy with characters are checked, and the matching random low-moment theorem is stated.

proof_stages.label: Random reduction from sums to Euler-product integrals
proof_stages.summary: The random proof first conditions on small-prime values and uses the unique large-prime factorization of many integers to bound the low moment of the sum by x^q times a q-th moment of a square-average of the associated Euler product.

proof_stages.label: Critical-chaos estimate for Euler products
proof_stages.summary: The remaining random Euler-product integral is controlled by imposing growth restrictions on partial Euler products, a probabilistic critical-multiplicative-chaos step that supplies the log-log saving beyond Hölder.

proof_stages.label: Failure of direct character conditioning
proof_stages.summary: Trying to imitate random conditioning by fixing individual values chi(p) in arcs fails for moderately large x because the random model has atoms far smaller than 1/r while the finite character family has probability granularity about 1/r.

proof_stages.label: Coarse Euler-product conditioning and transfer to characters
proof_stages.summary: The proof avoids the granularity problem by truncating the primes without changing log-log size, conditioning only on a mesh of partial Euler products, matching their mixed moments with the random model, and then applying the random bound.

proof_stages.label: Lower-bound problem and theta nonvanishing application
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by explaining that matching lower bounds remain open, that current combinatorial lower bounds are much weaker, and that such lower bounds would imply positive-proportion nonvanishing for Dirichlet theta values where mollification seems unavailable.

examples.name: Principal character and Legendre symbol
examples.note: These two standard Dirichlet characters make the definition concrete and explain why the principal character is excluded in nontrivial cancellation questions.
examples.engagement_reason: the principal character and Legendre symbol are defined and cited as examples of Dirichlet characters, without checking the axioms or computing with them

passes.reason: The principal character is the trivial constant-on-units character and is the excluded no-cancellation enemy, while the Legendre symbol is a standard quadratic character.
The principal character is the trivial identity character and the excluded no-cancellation case, while the Legendre symbol is a standard nonprincipal comparator.

The principal character is the trivial identity character and is used to show why cancellation statements cannot include all characters, while the Legendre symbol is only a standard nontrivial example.

examples.name: Half-period sums x = r/2
examples.note: The case of sums of length comparable to the modulus illustrates both the classical Pólya-Vinogradov/Montgomery-Vaughan regime and why the new denominator disappears when l = min(x,r/x) is bounded.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes to x = r/2, uses fourth- and second-moment bounds, and derives a positive proportion of sums of size about root r

passes.reason: The half-period sum is the symmetric fixed-proportion long-sum case in which square-root-size behavior is tight, not a refutation.
The choice x=r/2 is the symmetric half-period cut used as a long-sum benchmark, not as a refutation.
The half-period length is a specially symmetric interval in the residue period, used as a benchmark where root-size sums really occur.
The half-period length x=r/2 is used as a representative fixed-proportion long-sum benchmark, not as an intrinsically extremal or adversarial object.
the choice x=r/2 is used as a representative fixed-proportion long interval rather than as a structurally extreme object
The choice x=r/2 is used as a representative long character-sum length where root cancellation is typical, not as a failure case.
The choice x=r/2 is a self-complementary half-period long-sum case where the old moment bound is tight.

The length x=r/2 is a reflection-symmetric half-period used to illustrate known root-size behavior, not to refute a claim.
The half-period is a critical long-sum regime where the old moment bounds are tight, not an adversarial counterexample.
The choice x=r/2 is the reflection-symmetric half-period boundary where the long-sum bound is tight rather than a refutation.
The case x=r/2 is a boundary long-sum regime with bounded dual length where the square-root-size moment behavior is tight.

examples.name: The first absolute moment q = 1/2
examples.note: Substituting q = 1/2 in the theorem displays the more-than-square-root-cancellation conclusion in the simplest numerical form.
examples.engagement_reason: the theorem is specialized to q = 1/2 and the denominator is simplified to derive a first-absolute-moment bound below root x

examples.name: Modular-form turn-around at the square-root conductor
examples.note: An audience question about Booker/Katz and modular forms uses the expected reciprocal turn-around near the square-root conductor as an analogy for the x versus r/x duality.
examples.engagement_reason: the modular-form square-root-conductor turn-around is raised as an analogy and motivation, but no calculation or construction is carried out for modular forms

passes.reason: The square-root conductor is the self-dual functional-equation midpoint, and the observed failure to turn around is used to disprove modularity.

examples.name: Siegel/Landau-zero analogy
examples.note: The audience comparison with exceptional zeros clarifies that the low-moment saving reflects second-moment inflation by a small set of large sums, not a zero phenomenon.
examples.engagement_reason: the Siegel/Landau-zero comparison is discussed only as a vague analogy and explicitly not as the mechanism behind the result

passes.reason: A Landau-Siegel zero is a hypothetical zero near the edge of the zero-free region, invoked only as an analogy for rare bad cases.

examples.name: Random Euler product with natural boundary
examples.note: The audience recalls the standard random Euler-product fact that almost-sure square-root cancellation corresponds to convergence right of the critical line and a natural boundary at it.
